examples.title: Variables and Data Types Demo
examples.description: Demonstrates variable declaration, different data types, and type checking in Python
examples.code:
# Variable declaration and assignment
name = "Alice"           # str
age = 20                 # int
height = 5.6             # float
is_student = True        # bool
grades = [85, 90, 78]     # list
info = {"course": "Python", "year": 2024}  # dict

# Type checking
print(type(name))        # <class 'str'>
print(type(age))         # <class 'int'>
print(type(height))     # <class 'float'>
print(type(is_student))  # <class 'bool'>

# Type conversion
num = 10
print(float(num))        # 10.0
print(str(num))          # "10"

# Boolean from non-boolean
print(bool(1))           # True
print(bool(""))          # False
examples.explanation: Python variables are dynamically typed. No type declaration needed. type() function returns the data type. Type conversion uses functions like int(), float(), str(), bool().

examples.title: Operators and Expressions
examples.description: Shows arithmetic, comparison, and logical operators with examples
examples.code:
# Arithmetic Operators
a, b = 10, 3
print(a + b)    # 13  (addition)
print(a - b)    # 7   (subtraction)
print(a * b)    # 30  (multiplication)
print(a / b)    # 3.333... (division - always float)
print(a // b)   # 3   (floor division)
print(a % b)    # 1   (modulo/remainder)
print(a ** b)   # 1000 (exponentiation)

# Comparison Operators
x, y = 5, 10
print(x == y)   # False (equal)
print(x != y)   # True  (not equal)
print(x < y)    # True  (less than)
print(x > y)    # False (greater than)
print(x <= y)   # True  (less or equal)
print(x >= y)   # False (greater or equal)

# Logical Operators
p, q = True, False
print(p and q)  # False
print(p or q)   # True
print(not p)    # False
examples.explanation: Arithmetic operators follow PEMDAS precedence. Comparison operators return boolean values. Logical operators use short-circuit evaluation - stops when result is determined.

examples.title: Control Flow with Indentation
examples.description: Demonstrates how indentation defines code blocks in Python
examples.code:
# If-elif-else (indentation critical)
temperature = 25

if temperature > 30:
    print("It's hot")
    print("Stay hydrated")
elif temperature > 20:
    print("Pleasant weather")
elif temperature > 10:
    print("Cool weather")
else:
    print("It's cold")

# For loop with range
for i in range(5):
    print(i, end=" ")  # 0 1 2 3 4

# While loop
count = 0
while count < 3:
    print(count)
    count += 1

# Function definition (indentation creates body)
def greet(name):
    """This function greets a person"""
    return f"Hello, {name}!"

print(greet("World"))
examples.explanation: Indentation (typically 4 spaces) defines code blocks. All statements at same indentation level belong to same block. Inconsistent indentation causes IndentationError. This is a key syntactic difference from brace-based languages.

examples.title: Truthy and Falsy Values Demo
examples.description: Demonstrates boolean evaluation of different values in Python
examples.code:
# Falsy values
falsy_values = [None, False, 0, 0.0, '', [], {}, set(), ()]

for val in falsy_values:
    print(f"{val!r} -> bool: {bool(val)}")

# Truthy values
truthy_values = [1, -1, 0.1, 'text', [1], {'a': 1}, {0}]

for val in truthy_values:
    print(f"{val!r} -> bool: {bool(val)}")

# Practical usage - default values
name = ""  # empty string
default_name = name or "Guest"
print(f"Welcome, {default_name}")  # Guest

# Short-circuit evaluation
x = 0
result = x and "never printed"  # Returns 0
print(f"and result: {result}")

y = 5
result = y or "default"  # Returns 5
print(f"or result: {result}")
examples.explanation: Python evaluates values implicitly in boolean contexts. Understanding truthy/falsy helps write cleaner code using short-circuit operators. Empty collections and zero values are falsy.
